summary: Gruber uses the announcement of a future reversible USB plug to illustrate what makes Apple distinctive as a company. The USB Promoter Group announced a reversible connector specification due by mid-2014 with products expected by 2016, but Apple's Lightning connector had already been shipping since 2012. Gruber argues that Lightning epitomizes Apple's philosophy: delivering elegant, superior solutions on its own timeline, regardless of industry standards. He acknowledges both sides of the debate — fans see elegance and convenience, critics see expensive proprietary lock-in — and considers both perspectives valid.
key_points: The USB industry announced a reversible plug that won't ship until 2016, four years after Apple shipped Lightning
Lightning epitomizes Apple's willingness to go proprietary when it believes the standard solution is inferior
Both the pro-Apple and anti-Apple interpretations of Lightning have legitimate merit
Apple's core philosophy is indifference to what the rest of the industry is doing, which is simultaneously its greatest strength and most common criticism
money_quotes: The Lightning adapter epitomizes what makes Apple Apple.
To the company's fans, it provides elegance and convenience — it's just so much nicer than micro-USB.
To the company's detractors, it exists to sell $29 proprietary adapters and to further enable Apple's fetish for device thinness.
Apple doesn't give a shit what everyone else is doing.
To some, that's what makes Apple great. To others, it's what's wrong with Apple.
Why in the world should we have to wait until 2016 to have a smaller, reversible plug?
Why in the world would you want a proprietary, non-standard, expensive plug?
key_insight: Lightning perfectly captures Apple's dual nature: the willingness to ship superior proprietary solutions years ahead of the industry, at the cost of locking users into an expensive, non-standard ecosystem.
